retrieved_memories.content:
Yeah, that’s the right order. Start with the simple stuff, not the service call.

Power first. Breaker, pilot if it’s gas. Then thermostat. Then make sure the shutoff is fully open. Look for any puddle or rust around the base and fittings. If it’s got a display, write the code down before you touch anything.

One thing I’d add: if it’s making noise, don’t just shrug it off. I had a furnace-and-pipe mess in winter once, and the little warning signs mattered more than the big failure did. Same idea here. Popping, rumbling, rusty water, breaker tripping over and over — that’s not “wait and see.”

If it’s older than about 10 years, I’d be suspicious anyway. These things don’t usually get better on their own.

Low-consequence thing with cheap parts? Patch it and keep moving. High-consequence thing — furnace, water heater, fridge, washer, anything that can leak or kill a whole day — I’d get less sentimental fast.

That’s the part people skip. They talk cost like it’s the whole story. It isn’t. A cheap repair on the wrong machine can still be expensive if it fails again at the worst time.

So yes, your filter is the right one.

retrieved_memories.content:
Pretty much, but that 30–50% rule is too neat. Depends what it is.

If it’s something dumb and cheap to patch — dryer belt, garbage disposal, maybe a dishwasher pump — I’d nurse it longer if the rest of it’s solid. If it’s a furnace, water heater, fridge, washer, anything that can turn into a bigger mess or a leak, I’d lean harder toward replacing before it takes something else with it.

What matters to me:
- age
- repair history
- whether parts are still easy to get
- whether the failure is cosmetic or can damage the house
- whether the energy use is getting stupid

I’ve done the “one more repair” game before. Sometimes it works. Sometimes you just keep feeding it until the thing dies at the worst possible time. So no, I wouldn’t just use a percentage and call it wisdom.

If you want a real answer, give me:
- appliance type
- approximate age
- what failed
- repair estimate
- replacement cost

Then I can tell you which side I’d take.

Yeah. That’s the part that matters.

What you laid out is the difference between managing a property and just collecting a stack of excuses. Access, change log, approval trail, exception log, reconciliation — that’s the right order. If those are clean, the rest usually tells on itself.

So yes — that’s the control layer I’d want before I stepped back.

retrieved_memories.content:
Pretty much the right lane. I’d want a little more on the controls, not just the paperwork.

What I’d add before I stepped back:
- **Who can spend what, without asking me first** — dollar limit for repairs, and when they have to call
- **Vendor list** — plumber, electrician, HVAC, roofer, handyman, with names and numbers
- **Before/after photos** for any repair over a set amount
- **Turnover checklist** — paint, flooring, appliances, smoke alarms, locks, water damage, signs of smoking
- **Reserve balance** — enough cash sitting there so they don’t start improvising
- **Communication rule** — monthly report, same day for anything legal, water, heat, or safety-related
- **Proof of inspections** — dates, initials, photos, not just “checked”

And one thing people skip: **access to the raw records**, not just a pretty owner summary. I want the lease, invoice, statement, and photos if I ask. Not a story.

If you want, I’d make this into a one-page handoff sheet with the exact items and reporting schedule.

Yeah, that’s the right order. Start with the simple stuff, not the service call.

Power first. Breaker, pilot if it’s gas. Then thermostat. Then make sure the shutoff is fully open. Look for any puddle or rust around the base and fittings. If it’s got a display, write the code down before you touch anything.

One thing I’d add: if it’s making noise, don’t just shrug it off. I had a furnace-and-pipe mess in winter once, and the little warning signs mattered more than the big failure did. Same idea here. Popping, rumbling, rusty water, breaker tripping over and over — that’s not “wait and see.”

If it’s older than about 10 years, I’d be suspicious anyway. These things don’t usually get better on their own.
